trial_name: 3Mg: Magnesium for Atrial Fibrillation after Cardiac Surgery (2013)
journal: The Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia
one_liner: A 3-hour intraoperative magnesium infusion did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

trial_name: The 65 Trial: MAP Targets in Older Adults with Septic Shock (2023)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among older adults with septic shock, a resuscitation strategy targeting a mean arterial pressure of 60 to 65 mm Hg did not significantly improve 90-day mortality compared with usual care.

trial_name: 6S: HES vs. Ringer's Acetate in Sepsis (2012)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with severe sepsis, resuscitation with hydroxyethyl starch 130/0.42 was associated with a significantly increased risk of death at 90 days and a greater need for renal-replacement therapy, as compared with resuscitation with Ringer's acetate.

trial_name: ABC: Awakening and Breathing Controlled Trial (2008)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Management with a daily SAT plus a SBT resulted in more ventilator-free days and a better 1-year survival than did management with sedation and weaning as directed by the clinical team.

trial_name: ACORN: Cefepime vs Piperacillin-Tazobactam in Hospitalized Adults (2023)
journal: the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among hospitalized adults in this randomized clinical trial, cefepime, compared with piperacillin-tazobactam, did not improve the primary outcome of the highest stage of acute kidney injury or death by day 14.

trial_name: ACURASYS: Neuromuscular Blockers in Early ARDS (2010)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Early administration of a neuromuscular blocking agent for 48 hours in patients with severe ARDS improved the adjusted 90-day survival and increased the number of ventilator-free days without increasing muscle weakness.

trial_name: ADRENAL: Hydrocortisone in Septic Shock (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with septic shock undergoing mechanical ventilation, a continuous infusion of hydrocortisone did not result in lower 90-day mortality than placebo.

trial_name: AID-ICU: Haloperidol for Delirium in ICU Patients (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Haloperidol had no significant effect on the number of days alive and out of the hospital at 90 days. We found no evidence of a beneficial effect of haloperidol on delirium.

trial_name: AKIKI: Timing of Renal-Replacement Therapy in AKI (2016)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a critically ill patient with severe acute kidney injury, a delayed strategy for initiating renal-replacement therapy was not associated with a significant difference in 60-day mortality, as compared with an early strategy.

trial_name: AKIKI2: RRT Timing in Less Severe AKI (2021)
journal: The American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill patients with severe AKI (KDIGO stage 2 or 3) and a high risk of progression, a more-delayed strategy for initiating RRT did not result in a significant difference in the number of days alive and free of RRT by day 28, as compared with a delayed strategy.

trial_name: ALBIOS: Albumin Replacement in Severe Sepsis (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with severe sepsis, albumin replacement in addition to crystalloids, as compared with crystalloids alone, did not improve survival at 28 and 90 days.

trial_name: ALVEOLI: Higher versus Lower PEEP in ARDS (2004)
primary_author: The Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome, a clinical trial of mechanical ventilation with higher levels of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) did not show a significant improvement in survival.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/PEEP, Recruitment & Driving Pressure/alveoli.json

trial_name: AMIKINHAL: Inhaled Amikacin for VAP Prevention (2019)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this randomized trial involving adults requiring mechanical ventilation for more than 72 hours, the administration of inhaled amikacin did not result in a lower incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia than placebo.

trial_name: The ANDROMEDA-SHOCK Investigators (2019)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with septic shock, a resuscitation strategy targeting normalization of capillary refill time, compared with a strategy targeting normalization of serum lactate levels, did not reduce 28-day mortality.

trial_name: ANZICS: Low-Dose Dopamine in Early Renal Dysfunction (2000)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: We have shown that, in critically ill patients at risk of acute renal failure, low-dose dopamine offers no benefit.

trial_name: APROCCHSS: Steroids for Septic Shock (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with septic shock, treatment with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone resulted in lower 90-day mortality compared to placebo.

trial_name: ARMA: Low Tidal Volumes for ARDS (2000)
primary_author: The Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome, a ventilation strategy that used lower tidal volumes than were traditionally used resulted in lower mortality and an increase in the number of days without ventilator use.

trial_name: ARISE: EGDT for Septic Shock (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In critically ill patients presenting to the emergency department with early septic shock, EGDT did not reduce all-cause mortality at 90 days, as compared with usual care.

trial_name: ART: Alveolar Recruitment for ARDS (2017)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with moderate to severe ARDS, a strategy of lung recruitment and titrated PEEP, compared with a conventional low-PEEP strategy, was associated with increased 28- day all-cause mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/PEEP, Recruitment & Driving Pressure/art.json

trial_name: ATHOS-3: Angiotensin II for Vasodilatory Shock (2017)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: The administration of angiotensin II to patients with vasodilatory shock resulted in a higher mean arterial pressure and a lower dose of norepinephrine than the administration of placebo.

trial_name: ATN: Intensity of Renal Support in Acute Kidney Injury (2008)
primary_author: The VA/NIH Acute Renal Failure Trial Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Intensive renal support in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury did not decrease mortality, improve recovery of kidney function, or reduce the rate of nonrenal organ failure as compared with less-intensive therapy.

trial_name: Andriulli et al: Early Enteral Nutrition in Acute Pancreatitis (2008)
journal: Digestive and Liver Disease
one_liner: In patients with acute pancreatitis, early nasoenteric feeding was not superior to a strategy of on-demand oral refeeding, as shown by the similar rates of complications and mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/andriulli-et-al-2008.json

trial_name: Annane et al: Steroids for Septic Shock (2002)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Treatment with low doses of hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone significantly reduced the risk of death in patients with septic shock and relative adrenal insufficiency.

trial_name: BICAR-ICU: Bicarbonate for Severe Acidemia (2018)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: In patients with severe metabolic Acidemia, sodium bicarbonate had no effect on the primary composite outcome. However, sodium bicarbonate decreased the primary composite outcome and 28-day mortality in the prespecified stratum of patients with acute kidney injury.

trial_name: BLING III: Beta-Lactam Infusion in Sepsis (2024)
journal: The Lancet Infectious Diseases
one_liner: Among critically ill patients with sepsis, the administration of beta-lactam antibiotics as a continuous infusion, compared with intermittent infusion, did not result in a lower risk of death at 90 days.

trial_name: BOUGIE: Bougie vs. Stylet for Emergency Intubation (2021)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill adults undergoing tracheal intubation, the use of a bougie did not significantly increase the incidence of successful intubation on the first attempt as compared with the use of an endotracheal tube with a stylet.

trial_name: BOX: BP and Oxygen Targets in Comatose Survivors of Cardiac Arrest (2022)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients who were comatose after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, targeting a mean arterial pressure of 63 mm Hg was not significantly different from targeting a mean arterial pressure of 77 mm Hg with respect to the risk of death or severe disability or coma at 90 days.

trial_name: BRACKEN I (NASCIS I): High-Dose Steroids in Spinal Cord Injury (1990)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that this trial provides no evidence to support the use of high-dose methylprednisolone in the first 48 hours after acute spinal cord injury.

trial_name: NASCIS II: High-Dose Steroids in Spinal Cord Injury (1990)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that methylprednisolone is an effective treatment for acute spinal-cord injury if it is given in the first eight hours after injury.

trial_name: NASCIS III: Duration of Steroid Therapy in Spinal Cord Injury (1997)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: We conclude that patients with acute spinal-cord injury who receive methylprednisolone within three hours of injury should be maintained on that treatment for 24 hours. When methylprednisolone is initiated between three and eight hours after injury, it should be continued for 48 hours.

trial_name: BaSICS: Balanced Solution vs. Saline in Critically Ill Patients (2021)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill patients, the use of a balanced solution, as compared with saline, did not reduce the risk of death at 90 days. The rate of infusion of crystalloids also had no effect on 90-day mortality.

trial_name: Bernard et al: Therapeutic Hypothermia after Cardiac Arrest (2002)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this randomized, controlled trial, we found that the induction of mild hypothermia in patients who remained unconscious after resuscitation from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest of presumed cardiac cause increased the proportion of patients who had a favorable neurologic outcome and were discharged home or to a rehabilitation facility.

trial_name: Besson et al: Beta-Blockade in Severe Sepsis (1995)
journal: Annales Françaises d'Anesthésie et de Réanimation
one_liner: In patients with severe sepsis, esmolol infusion effectively controlled heart rate but did not demonstrate a significant improvement in overall survival.

trial_name: Bouchard et al: Fluid Accumulation in AKI (2005)
journal: Kidney International
one_liner: Fluid overload is an independent risk factor for mortality in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury.

trial_name: Bozzette et al: Corticosteroids for Severe PCP in AIDS (1995)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that the early addition of corticosteroids to the therapeutic regimen for Pneumocystis pneumonia in patients with AIDS and moderate-to-severe impairment of oxygenation reduces the risks of respiratory failure and death.

trial_name: Brochard et al: Noninvasive Ventilation for COPD Exacerbations (1995)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that, in selected patients with acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, noninvasive ventilation can reduce the need for endotracheal intubation and decrease in-hospital mortality.

trial_name: CALORIES: Early Parenteral vs. Enteral Nutrition in Critical Illness (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We found no evidence of a difference in 30-day mortality between the parenteral-nutrition group and the enteral-nutrition group. There were also no significant differences in the rates of other clinically important outcomes.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/calories.json

trial_name: CAPE COD: Hydrocortisone in Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with severe community-acquired pneumonia being treated in the ICU, hydrocortisone reduced the risk of death at 28 days as compared with placebo.

trial_name: CARRESS-HF: Ultrafiltration in Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (2012)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute decompensated heart failure, cardiorenal syndrome, and persistent congestion, a strategy of stepped pharmacologic therapy was superior to a strategy of ultrafiltration for the preservation of renal function at 96 hours, with no significant difference in weight loss.

trial_name: CAST: Arrhythmia Suppression after Myocardial Infarction (1991)
primary_author: The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST) Investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: The results of the Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST) indicate that two such drugs, encainide and flecainide, do not improve survival in this group of patients and, in fact, are harmful.

trial_name: CATIS: Corticosteroids for Atrial Fibrillation after Cardiac Surgery (2007)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients undergoing cardiac surgery, a short course of perioperative corticosteroids did not result in a lower incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

trial_name: CATIS-1: Blood Pressure Reduction in Acute Ischemic Stroke (2014)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with acute ischemic stroke, immediate blood pressure reduction did not reduce the risk of death and major disability at 14 days or hospital discharge.

trial_name: Stroke (2022)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Among patients with acute ischaemic stroke who received intravenous thrombolysis, intensive blood pressure reduction did not significantly improve functional outcome at 90 days compared with the guideline-recommended standard blood pressure reduction.

trial_name: CENSER: Early Use of Norepinephrine in Septic Shock (2019)
journal: the American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: Early norepinephrine administration in patients with septic shock was associated with a higher rate of shock control.

trial_name: CESAR: ECMO for Severe Adult Respiratory Failure (2009)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: We conclude that transferring adult patients with severe but potentially reversible respiratory failure to a single centre specialising in the treatment of severe respiratory failure with ECMO is safe and, compared with conventional management, can improve survival without severe disability.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/ECMO & Advanced Ventilation Modes/cesar.json

trial_name: CHASTER: Hydrocortisone in Cardiac Surgery (2011)
journal: The Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia
one_liner: In patients undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, a single intraoperative high dose of hydrocortisone did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

trial_name: CHEST: HES vs. Saline in Critically Ill Adults (2012)
primary_author: John A. Myburgh, M.B., B.Ch., Ph.D.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a heterogeneous population of patients in the ICU, the use of 6% HES (130/0.4) did not reduce 90-day mortality, as compared with the use of saline.

trial_name: CHOICE: Hydrocortisone vs. Dexamethasone in COVID-19 ARDS (2023)
journal: The Lancet Respiratory Medicine
one_liner: In patients with COVID-19 and ARDS, treatment with dexamethasone, as compared with hydrocortisone, resulted in significantly more ventilator-free days at 21 days.

trial_name: CITRIS-ALI: Vitamin C in Sepsis and ARDS (2019)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In this preliminary randomized clinical trial of patients with sepsis and ARDS, a 96-hour infusion of vitamin C, compared with placebo, did not significantly improve the primary end point of SOFA scores. However, patients in the vitamin C group had significantly lower 28- day mortality.

trial_name: CLASSIC: Restrictive vs. Standard Fluid in Septic Shock (2022)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients with septic shock in the ICU, the use of a restrictive fluid strategy did not result in a lower 90-day mortality than the use of a standard fluid strategy.

trial_name: The CLOVERS Trial Investigators (2023)
primary_author: The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute Prevention and Early Treatment of Acute Lung Injury (PETAL) Clinical Trials Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with sepsis-induced hypotension, a restrictive fluid strategy was not associated with a significant difference in 90-day mortality as compared with a liberal fluid strategy.

trial_name: COCA: Corticosteroids in COVID-19 ARDS (2021)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with moderate to severe ARDS due to COVID-19, treatment with hydrocortisone, compared with no corticosteroids, did not significantly reduce 21-day all- cause mortality.

trial_name: COIITSS: Hydrocortisone in Severe Sepsis (2010)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients with severe sepsis, intravenous hydrocortisone did not reduce mortality at 28 days, irrespective of their response to corticotropin.

trial_name: COMMIT: Metoprolol in Acute Myocardial Infarction (2005)
primary_author: Zheng-Ming Chen, on behalf of the COMMIT collaborative group
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: In a wide range of patients with acute myocardial infarction, early intravenous then oral metoprolol was well tolerated but did not significantly reduce 28-day mortality.

trial_name: CORTICUS: Hydrocortisone in Septic Shock (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Hydrocortisone did not improve survival or reversal of shock in patients with septic shock, either overall or in patients who had a poor response to corticotropin. However, shock was reversed more quickly in patients treated with hydrocortisone.

trial_name: COVIDICUS: Hydrocortisone in Severe COVID-19 (2021)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among adults with severe COVID-19, treatment with hydrocortisone for 7 days did not significantly reduce 21-day all-cause mortality.

trial_name: CRASH-2: Tranexamic Acid in Bleeding Trauma Patients (2010)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: We conclude that tranexamic acid can be used in bleeding trauma patients to reduce the risk of death due to bleeding, with no evidence of an increase in vascular occlusive events.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/crash-2.json

trial_name: CRASH-3: Tranexamic Acid in Traumatic Brain Injury (2019)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Our results show that tranexamic acid is safe in patients with TBI and that treatment within 3 h of injury reduces head injury-related death. Treatment should be given as soon as possible after injury.

trial_name: CRASH: Corticosteroids in Significant Head Injury (2004)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Our results show that, in patients with head injury, corticosteroids should not be used routinely. The results of this trial provide no evidence that corticosteroid treatment is beneficial, and indeed suggest that it could be harmful.

trial_name: CRISTAL: Colloids vs. Crystalloids in Hypovolemic Shock (2013)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among ICU patients with hypovolemia, the use of colloids vs crystalloids did not result in a significant difference in 28-day mortality. Although 90-day mortality was lower among patients receiving colloids, this finding should be considered exploratory.

trial_name: CRYOSTAT-1: Early Cryoprecipitate in Major Trauma (2019)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with major trauma and bleeding, a prehospital transfusion of fibrinogen concentrate was feasible. However, the study was underpowered to detect a statistically significant difference in 30-day mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/cryostat-1.json

trial_name: CRYOSTAT-2: Early Cryoprecipitate for Major Haemorrhage in Trauma (2023)
journal: The Lancet Haematology
one_liner: Among adults with trauma and bleeding who were receiving a blood transfusion, the early administration of cryoprecipitate, as compared with standard care, did not significantly reduce 28-day all-cause mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/cryostat-2.json

trial_name: CULPRIT-SHOCK: PCI Strategy in Myocardial Infarction with Cardiogenic
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute myocardial infarction complicated by cardiogenic shock, the risk of a composite of death or severe renal failure at 30 days was lower among those who underwent PCI of the culprit lesion only than among those who underwent immediate multivessel PCI.

trial_name: DANAMI-2: Primary PCI in Acute Myocardial Infarction (2003)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that interhospital transfer of patients with STEMI for primary PCI is superior to on-site fibrinolysis.

trial_name: DAWN: Thrombectomy 6 to 24 Hours after Stroke (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with a clinical deficit that is disproportionately severe relative to the infarct volume, an extended time window for treatment with thrombectomy is beneficial.

trial_name: DE GANS 2002: Dexamethasone in Adults with Bacterial Meningitis (2002)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Early treatment with dexamethasone is associated with a reduction in the risk of an unfavorable outcome and, in particular, a reduction in the risk of death.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Neurological Critical Care/Other Neuro Critical Care Topics/de-gans-2002.json

trial_name: DECIMAL: Dexamethasone in COVID-19 with Moderate-to-Severe ARDS (2021)
journal: JAMA Internal Medicine
one_liner: In patients with moderate to severe ARDS due to COVID-19, dexamethasone treatment was not associated with a significant reduction in the number of ventilator-free days at 28 days.

trial_name: DECRA: Decompressive Craniectomy in Traumatic Brain Injury (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with severe diffuse traumatic brain injury and refractory intracranial hypertension, early decompressive craniectomy decreased intracranial pressure and the length of stay in the ICU but was associated with more unfavorable outcomes.

trial_name: DEFUSE 3: Thrombectomy 6 to 16 Hours after Stroke (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Endovascular thrombectomy for ischemic stroke 6 to 16 hours after a patient was last known to be well plus standard medical therapy resulted in better functional outcomes at 90 days and lower mortality than standard medical therapy alone.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/defuse-3.json

trial_name: DELCLAUX 2000: NIV for Post-Extubation Respiratory Failure (2000)
journal: the American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that noninvasive pressure support ventilation is an effective strategy for treating postextubation respiratory failure in selected patients, as it significantly reduces the need for reintubation and the duration of mechanical support.

trial_name: DESTINY II: Hemicraniectomy in Older Patients with Malignant MCA
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Although decompressive hemicraniectomy increased the number of patients who survived, it also increased the number of patients who survived with severe disability.

trial_name: DESTINY: Decompressive Hemicraniectomy in Malignant MCA Infarction (2007)
journal: Stroke
one_liner: The results of our pooled analysis suggest that early decompressive hemicraniectomy increases the probability of survival in patients with malignant middle-cerebral-artery infarction.

trial_name: DEVICE: Video vs. Direct Laryngoscopy in Critical Illness (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults undergoing tracheal intubation in an intensive care unit, the use of a video laryngoscope resulted in a significantly higher incidence of successful intubation on the first attempt than the use of a direct laryngoscope.

trial_name: Devlin et al: Cost-Effectiveness of a Sedation Protocol (2010)
journal: The American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: Implementation of a sedation protocol that targeted light sedation was associated with a significant reduction in the costs of care for adult ICU patients requiring mechanical ventilation for longer than 24 hours.

trial_name: DEXA-ARDS: Dexamethasone in Moderate to Severe ARDS (2020)
journal: The Lancet Respiratory Medicine
one_liner: In patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS, treatment with dexamethasone led to an increase in the number of ventilator-free days and a decrease in all-cause mortality.

trial_name: DIABOLO: Anakinra in Sepsis with Organ Failure (2021)
journal: JAMA Network Open
one_liner: In this randomized clinical trial of 137 patients with sepsis and organ failure, anakinra, compared with placebo, did not significantly improve organ function scores at day 7.

trial_name: DOSE: Diuretic Strategies in Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute decompensated heart failure, there were no significant differences in patients' global assessment of symptoms or in the change in renal function when diuretic therapy was administered by continuous infusion or by bolus injection twice daily or at a high dose or a low dose.

trial_name: DRAKULOVIC: Semirecumbent Position for Pneumonia Prevention (1999)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: The semirecumbent body position reduces frequency and risk of nosocomial pneumonia, especially in patients who receive enteral nutrition.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Neurological Critical Care/Other Neuro Critical Care Topics/drakulovic.json

trial_name: ECASS III: Thrombolysis for Stroke at 3 to 4.5 Hours (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute ischaemic stroke, intravenous alteplase administered between 3 h and 4.5 h after symptom onset significantly improved clinical outcomes.

trial_name: ECLS-SHOCK: ECMO in Myocardial Infarction with Cardiogenic Shock (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with acute myocardial infarction complicated by cardiogenic shock, early initiation of extracorporeal life support did not reduce the risk of death at 30 days as compared with early and complete revascularization.

trial_name: EDEN: Trophic vs. Full Enteral Feeding in Acute Lung Injury (2012)
primary_author: The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) ARDS Clinical Trials Network
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with acute lung injury, there was no significant difference in the number of ventilator-free days with initial trophic enteral nutrition compared with initial full enteral nutrition.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/eden-2012.json

trial_name: EGDT: Early Goal-Directed Therapy in Sepsis (2001)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that early goal-directed therapy provides significant benefits with respect to outcome in patients with severe sepsis and septic shock.

trial_name: ELAIN: Early vs. Late Initiation of RRT in Critical Illness (2016)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill patients with acute kidney injury, early initiation of renal replacement therapy was associated with a significant reduction in 90-day mortality.

trial_name: EOLIA: ECMO for Severe ARDS (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with very severe ARDS, 60-day mortality was not significantly lower with ECMO than with a strategy of conventional ventilation that included ECMO as rescue therapy.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/ECMO & Advanced Ventilation Modes/eolia.json

trial_name: EPO-TBI: Erythropoietin in Traumatic Brain Injury (2017)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Treatment with erythropoietin for 7 days did not result in an improvement in neurological outcome at 6 months in patients with moderate or severe traumatic brain injury.

trial_name: EPaNIC: Early vs. Late Parenteral Nutrition in Critical Illness (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Late initiation of parenteral nutrition was associated with a faster recovery and fewer complications, as compared with early initiation.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/epanic.json

trial_name: ESCAPE: Endovascular Treatment for Ischemic Stroke (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that in patients with acute ischemic stroke, rapid endovascular treatment improves functional outcome and reduces mortality.

trial_name: ESCORSELL 2016: Terlipressin vs Norepinephrine in Cirrhosis with Septic
journal: Gastroenterology
one_liner: In patients with cirrhosis and septic shock, terlipressin was not superior to norepinephrine in reversing shock at 6 hours, had more severe adverse effects, and did not improve survival.

trial_name: ETHICUS-2: Early Palliative Care in the ICU (2021)
journal: JAMA Internal Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill patients with a high risk of death, a proactive palliative care intervention did not significantly reduce the severity of PTSD symptoms at 6 months, but it did reduce the severity of anxiety and depression symptoms in family members.

trial_name: Esteban et al: Noninvasive Ventilation for Postextubation Respiratory Failure (2004)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation is not an effective treatment for unselected patients who have respiratory failure after extubation and that its use may delay required reintubation.

trial_name: EuroHYP-1: Prehospital Cooling after Cardiac Arrest (2019)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adults with coma after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, prehospital cooling with a rapid infusion of large-volume, ice-cold intravenous fluids did not improve neurologic outcome at 90 days.

trial_name: Eurotherm3235: Hypothermia for Intracranial Hypertension after TBI (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with an intracranial pressure of more than 20 mm Hg after traumatic brain injury, therapeutic hypothermia led to a worse outcome, as measured by the Extended Glasgow Outcome Scale, than standard care.

trial_name: ExPress: PEEP Setting in ARDS (2008)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients with ARDS, a multifaceted strategy aimed at maximizing alveolar recruitment and minimizing overdistention resulted in improved oxygenation and respiratory system compliance and a trend toward lower mortality, but it did not significantly reduce ventilator- free days.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/PEEP, Recruitment & Driving Pressure/express.json

trial_name: FACTT: Fluid Management in Acute Lung Injury (2006)
primary_author: The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) ARDS Clinical Trials Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Neither a liberal nor a conservative fluid-management strategy had a significant effect on the primary outcome of 60-day mortality. However, the conservative strategy improved lung function and shortened the duration of mechanical ventilation and intensive care.

trial_name: FAST: Focused Echocardiographic Evaluation in Life Support (2013)
journal: Academic Emergency Medicine
one_liner: In this multicenter, randomized, controlled trial, we found that the addition of a focused echocardiographic protocol to the early management of patients with undifferentiated shock did not improve survival or reduce the duration of organ support.

trial_name: FEAST: Fluid Boluses in Critically Ill African Children (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In critically ill children with impaired perfusion in a resource-limited setting in Africa, fluid boluses significantly increased 48-hour mortality.

trial_name: The FIRST-ABC Trial Investigators (2023)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill adults requiring mechanical ventilation, a strategy of analgesia first with remifentanil did not significantly improve the number of days alive and free of mechanical ventilation compared with a strategy of analgesia first with fentanyl.

trial_name: Failure (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and without hypercapnia, treatment with high-flow nasal oxygen, standard oxygen therapy, or noninvasive ventilation did not result in a significantly different intubation rate. However, mortality was lower in the high- flow-oxygen group than in the other two groups.

trial_name: FLUID: Hospital-Wide Lactated Ringer's versus Normal Saline (2025)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this cluster-randomized, crossover trial involving a heterogenous group of patients in academic and community hospitals, the use of lactated Ringer’s solution as compared with normal saline did not result in a significant difference in the incidence of death or readmission to the hospital within 90 days.

trial_name: HACA: Therapeutic Hypothermia after Cardiac Arrest (2002)
primary_author: The Hypothermia after Cardiac Arrest (HACA) Study Group
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients who have been successfully resuscitated after cardiac arrest due to ventricular fibrillation, therapeutic mild hypothermia increased the rate of a favorable neurologic outcome and reduced mortality.

trial_name: HALT-IT: Tranexamic Acid in Gastrointestinal Bleeding (2020)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: We found no evidence that tranexamic acid reduces death from gastrointestinal bleeding. Our results show an increased risk of venous thromboembolism and seizures, and do not support the use of tranexamic acid in patients with acute gastrointestinal bleeding.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/halt-it.json

trial_name: HAMLET: Hemicraniectomy in Malignant MCA Infarction (2009)
journal: The Lancet Neurology
one_liner: Although surgical decompression reduces the risk of death, about half of the survivors are left with moderately severe or severe disability. The decision to undertake surgical decompression should, therefore, be made on an individual basis and in discussion with patients' relatives.

trial_name: HOT-ICU: Oxygen Targets in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure (2021)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients in the ICU with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, there was no significant difference in 90-day mortality between patients assigned to a lower oxygenation target and those assigned to a higher oxygenation target.

trial_name: HYBERNATUS: Hypothermia for Severe Septic Shock (2015)
journal: The American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: In patients with septic shock, moderate hypothermia did not reduce 28-day mortality and was associated with a higher incidence of arrhythmia.

trial_name: HYPERION: Hypothermia for Non-shockable Cardiac Arrest (2019)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with coma after cardiac arrest with a nonshockable rhythm, therapeutic hypothermia at 33°C did not significantly reduce 90-day mortality as compared with targeted normothermia.

trial_name: HYPRESS: Hydrocortisone for the Prevention of Septic Shock (2016)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among adults with severe sepsis who were not in shock, a continuous infusion of hydrocortisone did not reduce the risk of septic shock at 14 days.

trial_name: IABP-SHOCK II: IABP in Myocardial Infarction with Cardiogenic Shock (2012)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Intra-aortic balloon counterpulsation did not significantly reduce 30-day mortality in patients with cardiogenic shock complicating acute myocardial infarction for whom an early revascularization strategy was planned.

trial_name: ICU-ROX: Conservative vs. Liberal Oxygen in Mechanical Ventilation (2019)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adults undergoing mechanical ventilation in the ICU, conservative oxygen therapy, as compared with usual oxygen therapy, did not significantly affect the number of ventilator- free days.

trial_name: IDEAL-ICU: Early vs. Delayed RRT in Septic Shock with AKI (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, we found no significant difference in 90-day mortality between an early and a delayed strategy for initiating renal replacement therapy in patients with septic shock and severe acute kidney injury.

trial_name: IMPROVE: Early vs. Delayed Enteral Nutrition in Critical Illness (2013)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In critically ill patients with relative contraindications to early enteral nutrition, initiating early parenteral nutrition was not associated with a lower 28-day mortality rate than a strategy of delayed enteral nutrition.

trial_name: Aneurysms (2002)
primary_author: Andrew Molyneux, on behalf of the ISAT collaborative group
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: In patients with a ruptured intracranial aneurysm, for which endovascular coiling and neurosurgical clipping are therapeutic options, the outcome in terms of survival free of disability at 1 year is significantly better with endovascular coiling.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Neurological Critical Care/Other Neuro Critical Care Topics/isat.json

trial_name: ISIS-2: Streptokinase and Aspirin in Acute Myocardial Infarction (1988)
primary_author: The ISIS-2 (Second International Study of Infarct Survival) Collaborative Group
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Streptokinase alone and aspirin alone each produced a highly significant reduction in 5- week vascular mortality. The combination of streptokinase and aspirin was significantly better than either agent alone.

trial_name: IST-3: Thrombolysis for Ischemic Stroke within 6 Hours (2012)
primary_author: The IST-3 (Third International Stroke Trial) collaborative group
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: For patients with ischaemic stroke, the early hazards of thrombolysis are substantial, but are offset by later benefits. For every 1000 patients given alteplase within 6 h of stroke, despite about ten extra early deaths, about 30 more will be alive and independent at 6 months.

trial_name: ITACTIC: Viscoelastic Assay-Guided Resuscitation in Major Trauma (2021)
primary_author: K. Baksaas-Aasen, on behalf of the ITACTIC collaborators
journal: Intensive Care Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with trauma and major bleeding, transfusion guided by viscoelastic hemostatic assays, compared with transfusion guided by conventional coagulation tests, did not result in a statistically significant improvement in the primary outcome of survival free of massive transfusion at 24 hours.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/itactic.json

trial_name: IVOIRE: High-Volume Hemofiltration in Septic Shock (2013)
journal: Intensive Care Medicine
one_liner: In this multicenter randomized controlled trial, we did not find a significant reduction in 28- day mortality with high-volume hemofiltration as compared with standard-volume hemofiltration in patients with septic shock and acute kidney injury.

trial_name: Jones et al: Preemptive Resuscitation in Intermediate-Risk Sepsis (2017)
journal: The American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with suspected infection and an intermediate lactate level, a preemptive resuscitation strategy did not reduce the incidence of worsening organ dysfunction at 72 hours.

trial_name: Kress et al: Daily Interruption of Sedative Infusions (2000)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that daily interruption of sedative-drug infusions in critically ill patients receiving mechanical ventilation decreases the duration of mechanical ventilation and the length of stay in the intensive care unit.

trial_name: Kumar et al: Duration of Hypotension Before Antibiotics in Septic Shock (2006)
journal: Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: Effective antimicrobial administration within the first hour of documented hypotension was associated with a survival rate of 79.9%. Each hour of delay in antimicrobial administration over the ensuing 6 hours was associated with an average decrease in survival of 7.6%.

trial_name: LAU: Laryngeal Mask Airway in the Unconscious Patient (2000)
journal: the Annals of Emergency Medicine
one_liner: In non-arrested, unconscious patients requiring prehospital airway management, the laryngeal mask airway is a more effective and faster method for securing the airway compared to conventional endotracheal intubation when performed by paramedics.

trial_name: LEUVEN I: Intensive Insulin Therapy in Critical Illness (2001)
primary_author: Greet Van den Berghe, M.D., Ph.D.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We conclude that intensive insulin therapy to maintain normoglycemia substantially reduces mortality and morbidity among patients in the surgical intensive care unit.

trial_name: LEUVEN II: Intensive Insulin Therapy in the Medical ICU (2006)
primary_author: Greet Van den Berghe, M.D., Ph.D.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this randomized, controlled study, we found that intensive insulin therapy to maintain normoglycemia did not reduce in-hospital mortality among critically ill patients in the medical intensive care unit but did result in a significant increase in the risk of severe hypoglycemia.

trial_name: LOVIT: Vitamin C in Sepsis (2022)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In adult patients with sepsis receiving vasopressor therapy in the ICU, treatment with an intravenous infusion of vitamin C did not lead to a lower risk of death or persistent organ dysfunction at 28 days than treatment with placebo.

trial_name: LOVS: Low vs. Higher PEEP for Acute Lung Injury (2008)
primary_author: The LOVS (Lung Open Ventilation Study) Investigators.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with ARDS, a ventilation strategy using higher PEEP and recruitment maneuvers did not reduce mortality compared to a strategy using lower PEEP.

trial_name: Lauzier et al: Anemia and Outcomes in Traumatic Brain Injury (2008)
journal: Critical Care Medicine
one_liner: In this cohort of patients with moderate to severe TBI, we observed that anemia was frequent and independently associated with poor neurologic outcome, whereas RBC transfusion was not.

trial_name: MAPPET-3: Thrombolysis in Intermediate-Risk Pulmonary Embolism (2002)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with acute pulmonary embolism without arterial hypotension and shock, thrombolysis can prevent the escalation of treatment and is associated with a good outcome.

trial_name: MARIK 2017: Vitamin C, Hydrocortisone, and Thiamine in Sepsis (2017)
journal: Chest
one_liner: Our results suggest that the early use of intravenous vitamin C, together with corticosteroids and thiamine, are effective in preventing progressive organ dysfunction, including acute kidney injury, and in reducing the mortality of patients with severe sepsis and septic shock.

trial_name: MASH-2: Methylprednisolone in Severe Alcoholic Hepatitis (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis, treatment with methylprednisolone was not associated with a significant reduction in 90-day mortality.

trial_name: MEDURI 2007: Prolonged Methylprednisolone in ARDS (2007)
journal: Chest
one_liner: In patients with severe early ARDS, prolonged administration of methylprednisolone was associated with a significant improvement in pulmonary and multiple-organ dysfunction and a reduction in mortality.

trial_name: MENDS: Dexmedetomidine vs. Lorazepam for Sedation in Sepsis (2008)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among mechanically ventilated patients with sepsis, a strategy of sedation with dexmedetomidine resulted in more days alive without delirium or coma and a lower prevalence of delirium as compared with a strategy of sedation with lorazepam.

trial_name: MENDS2: Dexmedetomidine vs. Propofol in Sepsis (2016)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults with sepsis undergoing mechanical ventilation, sedation with dexmedetomidine, compared with propofol, did not result in a statistically significant difference in 90-day mortality.

trial_name: MIND-USA: Antipsychotics for Delirium in Critical Illness (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients in the ICU with acute respiratory failure or shock and hypoactive or hyperactive delirium, treatment with haloperidol or ziprasidone, compared with placebo, did not significantly alter the duration of delirium.

trial_name: MINT: Transfusion Strategies for Myocardial Infarction and Anemia (2021)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia, a liberal transfusion strategy did not significantly reduce the risk of recurrent myocardial infarction or death at 30 days.

trial_name: Adapted from the MOPETT Investigators (2012)
journal: The Journal of Thrombosis and Thrombolysis
one_liner: In patients with moderate pulmonary embolism, a lower, “safe dose” of tissue plasminogen activator was associated with a significant improvement in pulmonary artery pressures and a reduction in the combined outcome of death or recurrent PE, without an increase in major bleeding.

trial_name: Magpie: Magnesium Sulphate for Pre-eclampsia (2002)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Magnesium sulphate is a safe and effective treatment for women with pre-eclampsia. It more than halves the risk of eclampsia and probably reduces the risk of maternal death.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Neurological Critical Care/Other Neuro Critical Care Topics/magpie-2002.json

trial_name: Morelli et al: Esmolol in Septic Shock (2013)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients with septic shock, targeting a heart rate of 80 to 94 beats/min with esmolol was associated with a reduction in heart rate to the target range without adverse effects and with a reduction in mortality.

trial_name: The NICE-SUGAR Study Investigators (2009)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this large, international, randomized trial, we found that intensive glucose control increased mortality among adults in the ICU.

trial_name: NICO: Noninvasive Airway Management in Comatose Poisoned Patients (2023)
journal: the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among comatose patients with suspected acute poisoning, a conservative strategy of withholding intubation was associated with a greater clinical benefit for the composite end point of in-hospital death, length of ICU stay, and length of hospital stay.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/PEEP, Recruitment & Driving Pressure/nico-2023.json

trial_name: NICO (EPVent2): Esophageal Pressure-Guided PEEP in ARDS (2017)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with moderate to severe ARDS, a strategy of titrating PEEP to a positive transpulmonary pressure did not significantly improve physiological outcomes or mortality compared with an empiric high-PEEP strategy.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/PEEP, Recruitment & Driving Pressure/nico-epvent2-2017.json

trial_name: NIEWOEHNER 1999: Systemic Steroids for COPD Exacerbations (1999)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients hospitalized for an exacerbation of COPD, treatment with systemic corticosteroids improves clinical outcomes and shortens the hospital stay.

trial_name: NINDS: t-PA for Acute Ischemic Stroke (1995)
primary_author: The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) rt-PA Stroke Study Group
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Despite an increased incidence of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage, treatment with intravenous t-PA within three hours of the onset of ischemic stroke improved clinical outcome at three months.

trial_name: NONSEDA: No Sedation in Mechanically Ventilated Patients (2010)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Our results challenge the widespread practice of sedating all hemodynamically stable patients during mechanical ventilation.

trial_name: NORDISTEMI: Therapeutic Hypothermia in STEMI (2016)
journal: The Journal of the American College of Cardiology
one_liner: In this randomized clinical trial of 200 patients with STEMI, prehospital cooling with cold saline infusion did not significantly reduce infarct size.

trial_name: NUTRIREA-3: Early Enteral vs. Parenteral Nutrition in Shock (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults with circulatory shock, early enteral nutrition did not result in a lower 90-day mortality than early parenteral nutrition.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/nutrirea-3.json

trial_name: OPTIMISE: Cardiac Output-Guided Hemodynamic Therapy in High-Risk
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among high-risk patients undergoing major gastrointestinal surgery, a cardiac output– guided hemodynamic therapy algorithm, compared with usual care, did not significantly reduce a composite of complications and 30-day mortality.

trial_name: OSCAR: High-Frequency Oscillation in ARDS (2013)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with ARDS, the use of high-frequency oscillatory ventilation, as compared with conventional mechanical ventilation, does not reduce, and may increase, mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/ECMO & Advanced Ventilation Modes/oscar.json

trial_name: OSCILLATE: High-Frequency Oscillation in ARDS (2013)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this trial of patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS, early application of HFOV, as compared with a conventional-ventilation strategy that included high PEEP and recruitment maneuvers, did not reduce, and may have increased, in-hospital mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/ARDS & Mechanical Ventilation/ECMO & Advanced Ventilation Modes/oscillate.json

trial_name: PAC-Man: Pulmonary Artery Catheters in Critical Care (2005)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: We found no evidence that management with a PAC, as compared with standard care without a PAC, affects mortality or morbidity in a general population of critically ill adults.

trial_name: PATCH: Platelet Transfusion in Traumatic Brain Injury (2023)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among adult patients with traumatic brain injury and antiplatelet therapy use, prophylactic platelet transfusion, compared with standard care, was associated with a higher odds of death at 6 months.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/patch.json

trial_name: PEERLESS: Impella vs. ECMO in Cardiogenic Shock (2024)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with acute myocardial infarction and cardiogenic shock, there was no significant difference between early initiation of Impella CP and early initiation of VA- ECMO with regard to 30-day mortality.

trial_name: PEITHO: Thrombolysis in Intermediate-Risk Pulmonary Embolism (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism, fibrinolytic therapy prevented hemodynamic decompensation but increased the risk of major hemorrhage and stroke.

trial_name: SuDDICU: Selective Decontamination of the Digestive Tract (2022)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among mechanically ventilated adults in the ICU, the use of selective decontamination of the digestive tract did not result in a significantly lower 90-day mortality rate than standard care.

trial_name: PLUS: Balanced Solution vs. Saline in Critically Ill Adults (2022)
primary_author: Simon Finfer, M.D., on behalf of the PLUS Investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults, the use of a balanced multielectrolyte solution (Plasma-Lyte 148), as compared with saline, did not lead to a lower risk of death at 90 days.

trial_name: POISE: Perioperative Beta-Blockade in Noncardiac Surgery (2008)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: In patients undergoing non-cardiac surgery, metoprolol succinate extended-release was associated with a reduced risk of myocardial infarction but an increased risk of death and stroke.

trial_name: PRACTICE: Procalcitonin to Guide Antibiotic Duration in the ICU (2024)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults receiving empirical antibacterial therapy in the ICU, a procalcitonin-guided strategy for discontinuing therapy was not associated with a lower risk of death at 90 days than standard care.

trial_name: PRECISe: Personalised Resuscitation in Sepsis-Induced Hypotension (2023)
journal: The Lancet Respiratory Medicine
one_liner: Among adults with sepsis-induced hypotension in the emergency department, a personalised resuscitation protocol guided by capillary refill time, compared with usual care, did not significantly reduce the composite of all-cause mortality or persistent organ dysfunction at 30 days.

trial_name: PREPARE II: IV Fluid Bolus before Tracheal Intubation (2023)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill adults undergoing tracheal intubation, the administration of an intravenous fluid bolus did not significantly decrease the incidence of cardiovascular collapse.

trial_name: PREVENT: Pantoprazole vs. Placebo for Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis (2019)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients in the ICU who were at risk for gastrointestinal bleeding, mortality at 90 days and the incidence of clinically important events were not significantly lower with pantoprazole than with placebo.

trial_name: PRODEX: Dexmedetomidine vs. Standard Sedatives in the ICU (2012)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among long-term mechanically ventilated ICU patients, sedation with dexmedetomidine was not inferior to standard care in maintaining light to moderate sedation and resulted in a shorter time to extubation and a lower likelihood of delirium.

trial_name: PROPPR: Transfusion Ratios in Severe Trauma (2015)
primary_author: John B. Holcomb, M.D., on behalf of the PROPPR Study Group
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients with severe trauma and major bleeding, we found no significant differences in 24-hour or 30-day mortality between patients receiving blood products in a 1:1:1 ratio and those receiving them in a 1:1:2 ratio. However, more patients in the 1:1:1 group achieved hemostasis and fewer experienced death due to exsanguination by 24 hours.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/proppr.json

trial_name: PROSEVA: Prone Positioning in Severe ARDS (2013)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with severe ARDS, early application of prolonged prone-positioning sessions significantly decreased 28-day mortality.

trial_name: PROTECT: LMWH vs. Unfractionated Heparin for VTE Prophylaxis (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this large randomized trial, we found that, among medical–surgical critically ill patients, dalteparin was not superior to unfractionated heparin in reducing the incidence of proximal deep-vein thrombosis but did reduce the incidence of pulmonary embolism.

journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill patients with COVID-19, intermediate-dose prophylactic anticoagulation, compared with standard-dose prophylactic anticoagulation, did not result in a significant difference in the primary composite outcome of venous or arterial thrombosis, treatment with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, or mortality within 30 days.

trial_name: PROTRATA: Prone Positioning in Trauma Patients with ARDS (2021)
journal: Anesthesiology
one_liner: In patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS due to trauma, prone positioning did not significantly decrease 28-day mortality.

trial_name: PROWESS: Drotrecogin Alfa (Activated) in Severe Sepsis (2001)
primary_author: Gordon R. Bernard, M.D., on behalf of the PROWESS Study Group
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Treatment with drotrecogin alfa activated reduced the 28-day mortality rate from 30.8 percent in the placebo group to 24.7 percent in the drotrecogin alfa activated group, a relative reduction in the risk of death of 19.4 percent (P=0.005).

trial_name: PermiT: Permissive Underfeeding in Critically Ill Patients (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this randomized trial, we found no significant difference in 90-day mortality between critically ill adults who received a moderate amount of calories from enteral nutrition and those who received a full amount of calories.

trial_name: Pickard et al: Nimodipine in Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (1989)
primary_author: J. D. Pickard, on behalf of the trial group
journal: The British Medical Journal (BMJ)
one_liner: This study provides evidence that nimodipine reduces the occurrence of cerebral infarction and poor outcome after subarachnoid haemorrhage.

trial_name: ProCESS: Protocolized Care for Early Septic Shock (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a multicenter trial in the United States, we found that protocol-based resuscitation of patients in whom septic shock was diagnosed in the emergency department did not improve outcomes.

trial_name: ProMISe: Protocolised Management in Sepsis (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this multicenter trial in England, we found that early goal-directed therapy did not improve outcomes in patients with septic shock and led to an increase in health care costs.

trial_name: RECOVERY: Dexamethasone in Hospitalized Patients with Covid-19 (2020)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine (preliminary report released June 2020)
one_liner: In patients hospitalized with Covid-19, the use of dexamethasone resulted in lower 28-day mortality among those who were receiving either invasive mechanical ventilation or oxygen alone at randomization but not among those receiving no respiratory support.

trial_name: REDOXS: Glutamine and Antioxidants in Critical Illness (2013)
primary_author: Daren Heyland, M.D., on behalf of the REDOXS Study Investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In critically ill adults with multiorgan failure, we found that 28-day mortality was significantly higher among patients who received glutamine than among those who did not. Supplementation with antioxidants alone or with glutamine and antioxidants had no effect on mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/redoxs.json

trial_name: REGARD-VAP: Routine Gastric Aspiration in Ventilated Patients (2020)
journal: JAMA Internal Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients receiving mechanical ventilation, a strategy of not monitoring gastric residual volume was not inferior to a strategy of routine monitoring of gastric residual volume with regard to the incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia.

trial_name: REMAP-CAP: IL-6 Antagonists in Critically Ill Patients with Covid-19 (2021)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In critically ill patients with Covid-19 receiving organ support in intensive care, treatment with the interleukin-6 receptor antagonists tocilizumab and sarilumab improved outcomes, including survival.

trial_name: REMATCH: LVADs for End-Stage Heart Failure (2001)
primary_author: Eric A. Rose, M.D., on behalf of the REMATCH investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: The use of a left ventricular assist device in patients with advanced heart failure resulted in a clinically meaningful survival benefit and an improved quality of life. A left ventricular assist device is an acceptable alternative therapy in selected patients who are not candidates for cardiac transplantation.

trial_name: RENAL: Intensity of Continuous Renal-Replacement Therapy in Critical
primary_author: The RENAL Replacement Therapy Study Investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this multicenter, randomized trial, we found no significant difference in 90-day mortality between critically ill patients with acute kidney injury who were treated with high-intensity or less-intensive continuous renal-replacement therapy.

trial_name: RESCUEicp: Decompressive Craniectomy in Traumatic Brain Injury (2016)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Our trial showed that decompressive craniectomy was associated with lower mortality than ongoing medical care. However, it was also associated with higher rates of vegetative state, lower severe disability, and upper severe disability; rates of moderate disability and good recovery were similar in the two groups.

trial_name: Failure (2018)
primary_author: James J. McNamee, Ph.D., on behalf of the REST Investigators
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, the use of extracorporeal carbon dioxide removal to facilitate ultra-protective lung ventilation did not significantly reduce 90- day mortality.

trial_name: REVISE: Vasopressin vs. Norepinephrine in Septic Shock (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adults with septic shock, treatment with vasopressin plus norepinephrine, compared with norepinephrine alone, did not significantly improve 90-day mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/revise-2023.json

trial_name: REVISE: Pantoprazole vs Placebo for Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis (2024)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among adult patients undergoing mechanical ventilation in the ICU, pantoprazole resulted in a significantly lower risk of clinically important gastrointestinal bleeding than placebo but did not result in a significant difference in the risk of 90-day mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/revise-2024.json

trial_name: ROSE: Neuromuscular Blockers in Early ARDS (2019)
primary_author: The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute Prevention and Early Treatment of Acute Lung Injury (PETAL) Clinical Trials Network
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS, there was no significant difference in 90- day mortality between patients who received a continuous infusion of cisatracurium and those who were treated with a usual-care approach with lighter sedation targets and no routine neuromuscular blockade.

trial_name: The RePHILL Trial Investigators (2022)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with trauma-related hemorrhagic shock, prehospital resuscitation with red blood cells and lyophilized plasma did not result in a significant difference in a composite of mortality or lactate clearance at 2 hours, as compared with resuscitation with saline.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/rephill.json

trial_name: SAFE: Saline versus Albumin Fluid Evaluation in Critical Illness (2004)
primary_author: The Saline versus Albumin Fluid Evaluation (SAFE) Study Investigators
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a heterogeneous group of patients in the ICU, the use of either 4 percent albumin or normal saline for fluid resuscitation resulted in similar outcomes at 28 days.

trial_name: SALT-ED: Balanced Crystalloids vs Saline in the ED (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among noncritically ill adults treated with intravenous fluids in the emergency department, there was no difference in hospital-free days between treatment with balanced crystalloids and treatment with saline.

trial_name: SEDCOM: Dexmedetomidine vs Midazolam for Sedation (2009)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In mechanically ventilated medical and surgical ICU patients, dexmedetomidine was as effective as midazolam in maintaining light to moderate sedation, with a lower prevalence of delirium and a shorter time to extubation.

trial_name: SEPSISPAM: High vs Low MAP Targets in Septic Shock (2014)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Targeting a mean arterial pressure of 80 to 85 mm Hg, as compared with 65 to 70 mm Hg, in patients with septic shock did not result in a significant difference in 28-day mortality.

trial_name: SETPOINT2: Early vs Standard Tracheostomy in Severe Stroke (2022)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with severe stroke receiving mechanical ventilation, a strategy of early tracheostomy, compared with a standard approach to tracheostomy, did not significantly improve the rate of survival without severe disability at 6 months.

trial_name: SHOCK: Early Revascularization for Cardiogenic Shock (1999)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Although a strategy of initial medical stabilization with intra-aortic balloon counterpulsation and subsequent revascularization did not result in a significant difference in 30-day mortality, as compared with a strategy of initial medical stabilization alone, the mortality rates were lower at 6 months in the group assigned to early revascularization.

trial_name: SIC: Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis in the ICU (1999)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In this large, randomized trial in a mixed population of critically ill patients, sucralfate was as effective as histamine-2-receptor antagonists for the prevention of clinically important gastrointestinal bleeding.

trial_name: SLEAP: Sedation Interruption vs. Protocolized Sedation (2012)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: For mechanically ventilated adults managed with protocolized sedation, the addition of daily sedation interruption did not reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation or ICU stay.

trial_name: SMART: Balanced Crystalloids vs Saline in the ICU (2018)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among critically ill adults, the use of balanced crystalloids for intravenous fluid administration resulted in a lower rate of the composite outcome of death from any cause, new renal-replacement therapy, or persistent renal dysfunction than the use of saline.

trial_name: SOAP II: Dopamine vs Norepinephrine in Shock (2010)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Although there was no significant difference in the rate of death between the dopamine and norepinephrine groups in the entire cohort, dopamine was associated with a higher rate of death at 28 days among patients with cardiogenic shock.

trial_name: SORT: Sildenafil for Improving Outcomes after V/V ECMO (2022)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In patients with severe ARDS requiring V/V ECMO, sildenafil did not significantly increase the number of ventilator-free days at day 28.

trial_name: SORT 1999: Terlipressin for Hepatorenal Syndrome (1999)
journal: Hepatology
one_liner: Terlipressin plus albumin is an effective therapy for hepatorenal syndrome, since it improves renal function and reverses the syndrome in a high proportion of patients.

trial_name: SPLIT: Saline vs. Plasma-Lyte in the ICU (2015)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In this randomized clinical trial of critically ill adults, we did not find a significant difference in the incidence of acute kidney injury between patients receiving a buffered crystalloid and those receiving saline.

trial_name: STARRT-AKI: Standard vs. Accelerated RRT in AKI (2020)
primary_author: The STARRT-AKI Investigators for the Canadian Critical Care Trials Group, the Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society Clinical Trials Group, and the United Kingdom Critical Care Research Group.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a large, multicenter, randomized trial, we found that an accelerated strategy for the initiation of renal-replacement therapy in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury did not decrease mortality at 90 days as compared with a standard strategy.

trial_name: STASH: Simvastatin in Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (2017)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, the use of simvastatin did not improve clinical outcomes at 6 months. These findings do not support the use of simvastatin for aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage.

trial_name: STICH II: Early Surgery for Lobar Intracerebral Hemorrhage (2013)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: The STICH II results confirm that early surgery does not increase the rate of death or disability at 6 months and might have a small but clinically relevant survival advantage for patients with spontaneous superficial intracerebral haemorrhage without intraventricular haemorrhage.

trial_name: STICH: CABG for Ischemic Heart Failure (2011)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In a cohort of patients with coronary artery disease and left ventricular dysfunction, there was no significant difference in the rate of death from any cause between patients who underwent medical therapy alone and those who underwent medical therapy plus CABG.

trial_name: STOPAH: Steroids or Pentoxifylline for Alcoholic Hepatitis (2015)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Pentoxifylline did not improve survival in patients with alcoholic hepatitis. Prednisolone was associated with a reduction in the risk of death at 28 days that was not statistically significant; however, the drug was associated with a significant reduction in 90-day and 1- year mortality.

trial_name: STORM: Sorafenib as Adjuvant Therapy for HCC (2015)
journal: The Lancet Oncology
one_liner: Adjuvant sorafenib did not improve recurrence-free survival, time to recurrence, or overall survival after surgical resection or local ablation in patients with hepatocellular carcinoma. The safety profile of sorafenib was consistent with previous reports.

trial_name: SUBIRÀ 2019: Helmet vs Face Mask NIV for Postextubation ARF (2019)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with postextubation respiratory failure at high risk of reintubation, noninvasive ventilation with a helmet resulted in a significantly lower reintubation rate and a significantly lower 90-day mortality rate than noninvasive ventilation with a face mask.

trial_name: SUP-ICU: Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis in the ICU (2018)
journal: The New England Jounal of Medicine
one_liner: In this large, multicenter trial involving adult patients in the ICU who were at risk for gastrointestinal bleeding, mortality at 90 days was not lower among those who received prophylactic pantoprazole than among those who received placebo.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/sup-icu.json

trial_name: SUPERNOVA: HFNC in Immunocompromised Patients (2018)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among immunocompromised patients with acute respiratory failure, a strategy of initial high-flow nasal cannula oxygen therapy compared with standard oxygen therapy did not significantly reduce the day-28 rate of tracheal intubation.

trial_name: Sprung et al: High-Dose Corticosteroids in Septic Shock (1984)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: We were unable to show an improvement in the overall survival of patients with severe, late septic shock who were treated with high-dose corticosteroids.

trial_name: TAME: Therapeutic Mild Hypercapnia after Cardiac Arrest (2022)
primary_author: The TAME Cardiac Arrest Trial Investigators.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients who were comatose after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, targeted therapeutic mild hypercapnia did not lead to better neurologic outcomes at 6 months than targeted normocapnia.

trial_name: TELSTAR: Transpulmonary Pressure-Guided PEEP in ARDS (2023)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among patients with moderate to severe ARDS, a strategy of setting PEEP levels based on transpulmonary pressure compared with a high PEEP-FiO2 table did not significantly improve 28-day mortality.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Nutrition, GI & Glycemic Control/Nutrition & GI Prophylaxis/telstar.json

trial_name: TEMKIN 1990: Prophylactic Phenytoin after Head Injury (1990)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Prophylactic phenytoin is effective in preventing post-traumatic seizures during the first week after severe head injury, but not thereafter.

trial_name: TICACOS: Tight Calorie Control in the ICU (2011)
journal: Intensive Care Medicine
one_liner: In this single-center pilot study a bundle comprising actively supervised nutritional intervention and providing near target energy requirements based on repeated energy measurements was achievable in a general ICU and may be associated with lower hospital mortality.

trial_name: TICH-2: Tranexamic Acid for Intracerebral Hemorrhage (2018)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: In patients with intracerebral haemorrhage, tranexamic acid was not associated with a significant reduction in the risk of death or dependency at 90 days, but it was associated with a reduction in early mortality and fewer serious adverse events.

trial_name: TRICC: Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion in the ICU (1999)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: A restrictive strategy of red-cell transfusion is at least as effective as and possibly superior to a liberal transfusion strategy in critically ill patients, with the possible exception of patients with acute myocardial infarction and unstable angina.

trial_name: TRICS-III: Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion in Cardiac Surgery (2017)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients undergoing cardiac surgery, a restrictive strategy for red-cell transfusion was noninferior to a liberal strategy with respect to the composite outcome of all-cause mortality, myocardial infarction, stroke, or new-onset renal failure with dialysis at hospital discharge or 28 days, whichever came first.

trial_name: Original Article: Restrictive versus Liberal Red-Cell Transfusion for Cardiac
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with septic shock, mortality at 90 days and rates of ischemic events and use of life support were similar among patients assigned to a hemoglobin threshold of 7 g per deciliter and those assigned to a threshold of 9 g per deciliter.

trial_name: TROICA: Thrombolysis in Cardiac Arrest (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, the use of tenecteplase during cardiopulmonary resuscitation did not result in a significantly higher rate of survival to hospital discharge than did the use of placebo.

trial_name: TTM 48: 24 vs 48 Hours of TTM after Cardiac Arrest (2017)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among adults with comatose out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, targeted temperature management for 48 hours did not improve 6-month neurologic outcomes compared with targeted temperature management for 24 hours.

trial_name: TTM: Targeted Temperature Management at 33°C vs 36°C (2013)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In unconscious survivors of out-of-hospital cardiac arrest of presumed cardiac cause, hypothermia at a targeted temperature of 33°C did not confer a benefit as compared with a targeted temperature of 36°C.

trial_name: TTM2: Targeted Temperature Management after Cardiac Arrest (2021)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with coma after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, targeted hypothermia did not lead to a lower incidence of death at 6 months than targeted normothermia.

trial_name: TracMan: Early vs Late Tracheostomy in the ICU (2013)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In this study of critically ill adult patients, a policy of early tracheostomy (within 4 days of admission) did not result in a lower 30-day mortality rate than a policy of late tracheostomy (after 10 days).

trial_name: ULTRA: Early vs Late Initiation of RRT in AKI (2016)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In critically ill patients with severe AKI, a strategy of delayed initiation of renal replacement therapy was not associated with a significant difference in 60-day mortality.
