trial_name: UPLIFT: Tiotropium in Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Long-term treatment with tiotropium was associated with improvements in lung function, health status, and exacerbations but did not significantly reduce the rate of decline in FEV1.

trial_name: VAM-IHCA: Vasopressin and Methylprednisolone for In-Hospital Cardiac
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: The combination of vasopressin and methylprednisolone during and after resuscitation and hydrocortisone in the postresuscitation period in patients with in-hospital cardiac arrest was associated with improved survival to hospital discharge with favorable neurologic outcome.

trial_name: VANISH: Vasopressin vs Norepinephrine in Septic Shock (2016)
primary_author: Anthony C. Gordon, M.B., B.S., M.D.
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: In this randomized clinical trial of adults with septic shock, the use of vasopressin in addition to norepinephrine did not improve the number of kidney failure-free days. However, vasopressin was not associated with an increased risk of serious adverse events.

trial_name: VASST: Vasopressin vs Norepinephrine in Septic Shock (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Low-dose vasopressin, as compared with norepinephrine, did not reduce mortality rates in patients with septic shock who were being treated with catecholamine vasopressors.

trial_name: VILLANUEVA: Restrictive vs Liberal Transfusion in Upper GI Bleeding (2013)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: As compared with a liberal transfusion strategy, a restrictive strategy significantly improved outcomes in patients with acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding.

trial_name: VISEP: Hydroxyethyl Starch and Intensive Insulin in Sepsis (2008)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: In patients with severe sepsis, resuscitation with hydroxyethyl starch as compared with Ringer's lactate resulted in a higher rate of acute kidney injury and a greater need for renal- replacement therapy. Intensive insulin therapy did not reduce mortality and was associated with a higher rate of severe hypoglycemia.

trial_name: WATERFALL: Early Liberal vs Restrictive Fluid in Septic Shock (2022)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Among patients with septic shock in the ICU, a strategy of restricted fluid administration did not significantly reduce 90-day mortality compared with a strategy of liberal fluid administration.

trial_name: WIND: Pressure Support vs T-Piece for Weaning (2022)
journal: The Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)
one_liner: Among critically ill adults undergoing a first spontaneous breathing trial, a trial with a low level of pressure support, as compared with a T-piece, resulted in a significantly greater likelihood of successful weaning at day 21.

trial_name: WOMAN: Tranexamic Acid for Postpartum Hemorrhage (2017)
journal: The Lancet
one_liner: Tranexamic acid reduces death due to bleeding in women with post-partum haemorrhage with no evidence of adverse effects. Tranexamic acid should be used in all women with post- partum haemorrhage.
file_path: esbicm_trials/Trauma, Hemorrhage, Coagulopathy & VTE/Hemorrhage Control & Resuscitation/woman.json

trial_name: WUNDERINK: Empiric Antibiotics for VAP (2012)
journal: Chest
one_liner: Among patients with VAP and an MDR risk factor, empiric combination therapy with antipseudomonal agents did not improve survival or clinical outcomes compared with antipseudomonal monotherapy. Empiric MRSA coverage was associated with improved survival in settings where MRSA is prevalent.

trial_name: YANG 1991: Predicting Weaning Outcome (1991)
primary_author: Karl L. Yang, M.D., and Martin J. Tobin, M.D.
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
one_liner: Rapid shallow breathing, as reflected by the f/VT ratio, was the most accurate predictor of failure, and its absence the most accurate predictor of success, in weaning patients from mechanical ventilation.
